Description: Broad liberal-arts education concentrated in observational and theoretical astronomy, with flexibility for coursework across other disciplines. Department of Physics and Astronomy.
Rigorous preparation for employment or graduate study in astrophysics and physics; requires stronger math and physics prerequisites than the B.A. in Astronomy.

Covers the full breadth of the life sciences through four concentrations: Biochemistry; Cell Biology and Genetics; Ecology and Evolutionary Biology; and Integrative Biology. Replaced the older Biochemistry and Cell Biology and EEB majors in 2024.

Interdisciplinary major bridging chemistry and physics — quantum mechanics, thermodynamics, spectroscopy, and the chemistry–physics interface. Joint with the Departments of Chemistry and Physics and Astronomy.

Foundation in general, analytical, biological, inorganic, organic, and physical chemistry with lab experience. The B.S. is American Chemical Society certified; the B.A. is more flexible for double majors and pre-professional paths.

Majors: Earth, Environmental and Planetary Sciences (B.A. or B.S.)
Description: Earth and planetary systems, plate tectonics, geochemistry, oceanography, and climate. The B.S. specializes in Geoscience, Environmental Earth Science, or Planetary Science.
Interdisciplinary major in human–environment interactions with concentrations in Earth Science and Ecology and Evolutionary Biology. Jointly administered by BioSciences and EEPS.

Foundation in health promotion and disease prevention, public and community health. Preparation for graduate study in public health, health education, medicine, or dentistry. Department of Kinesiology.
Pure and applied math from calculus and combinatorics through abstract algebra, analysis, differential geometry, topology, and PDEs.

Interdisciplinary study of the brain across biological, biochemical, computational, and behavioral perspectives. B.S. concentrations: Computational Neuroscience; Molecular and Cellular Neuroscience. Administered by BioSciences.
B.A. offers a broad liberal-arts physics education; B.S. prepares students rigorously for graduate study or research careers in physics and related fields.
Basic science applied to human movement, performance, and exercise. Preparation for graduate study in exercise science, physical therapy, or medicine. Department of Kinesiology.

College: George R. Brown School of Engineering and Computing
Description: Machine learning, deep learning, NLP, computer vision, robotics, and intelligent systems. Administered by the Department of Computer Science.
Bridges bioengineering, basic science, and clinical medicine, with translational research skills for moving biotech advances from bench to bedside. BSBE only — no B.A. option.

Majors: Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering (B.A. or BSChE)
Description: Fluid mechanics, heat and mass transfer, thermodynamics, kinetics, reactor design, process control, and product/process design. BSChE specializations: Biomolecular Engineering; Computational Engineering; Energy/Sustainability; Materials/Nanotechnology.

Majors: Civil and Environmental Engineering (B.A., BSCE, or BSEnvE)
Description: BSCE specializations: Environmental Engineering; Hydrology and Water Resources; Structural Engineering and Mechanics; Urban Infrastructure, Reliability, and Management. BSEnvE focuses on Sustainable Water; Air, Climate, and Energy; Resilient Infrastructure; and Environmental Management.
Foundations applicable to engineering, physical sciences, life sciences, social sciences, and CS — math underlying modern algorithms for data science and computation.
Algorithms, programs and programming, and computational systems. Faculty research spans algorithms and complexity, AI, compilers, distributed computation, graphics, NLP, and machine learning.

Majors: Electrical and Computer Engineering (B.A. or BSECE)
Description: Four areas: AI & Systems (signal/image processing, wireless, data analytics); Computer Engineering (architecture, embedded systems, VR); Neuroengineering (BCIs, computational neuroscience); Photonics, Electronics, and Nano-devices.

Majors: Materials Science and NanoEngineering (B.A. or BSMSNE)
Description: Processing, structure, properties, and performance of metals, semiconductors, ceramics, polymers, composites, biomaterials, and nanomaterials. The BSMSNE includes capstone design projects.

Aerospace, biomedical systems, computational fluid dynamics, mechanical design, mechanics, robotics, and systems dynamics and controls. BSME specializations: Aerospace; Computational Engineering; Mechanics/Dynamics; Thermal Fluids.
Models for decision-making in complex systems and optimization tools for logistics, healthcare, energy, and other domains. Department of Computational Applied Mathematics and Operations Research.
Probabilistic and statistical ideas, methods, and applications. The B.S. is intended for graduate study or research careers in statistics or related quantitative fields.
Studio art major with experimentation across drawing, painting, sculpture, photography, film, ceramics, and performance. Concentrations in Studio Art and Film and Photography.
History, theory, and criticism of visual art and architecture from ancient to contemporary times across global cultures, with strong training in visual analysis and research writing.

British and American literatures from medieval to contemporary, with emphases in race and ethnicity studies, feminist and gender studies, queer theory, postcolonial Anglophone literature, and visual/comparative media studies. Optional Creative Writing concentration in fiction, poetry, creative nonfiction, podcasting, and translation.
Critical research, communication, and writing on the human past across periods and geographies. Optional International Concentration and Honors Program with archival research and thesis.
French and Francophone language, literature, culture, and history, building proficiency in French alongside critical and intercultural skills.
German-language literatures and cultures, language study, and the cultural and intellectual history of the German-speaking world.
Literatures, languages, and cultures of the Hispanic and Lusophone worlds — Spain, Portugal, Latin America, and the Caribbean.
Epistemology, metaphysics, ethics, and the meaning of existence with strong emphasis on argument construction and engagement with the history of philosophy.

Mainline religious traditions and marginal/repressed religious currents in multicultural, transnational contexts through historical, social, cultural, psychological, philosophical, and cognitive lenses.
Interdisciplinary study of the histories, languages, literatures, religions, and cultures of East, Southeast, South, and Central Asia. Department of Transnational Asian Studies.
History, culture, politics, literature, and social experiences of Africa and people of African descent in the U.S. and the African diaspora.
Interdisciplinary study of the history, politics, culture, literature, and social dynamics of Latin America and Latinx communities in the U.S.

Majors: Study of Women, Gender, and Sexuality (B.A.)
Description: Interdisciplinary examination of the construction of gender and sexuality across history, culture, and society through feminist theory, queer theory, and humanities/social sciences scholarship.
History, analysis, and theorization of film, television, video art, the Internet, and expanded cinema, with a capstone applied senior workshop.
Interdisciplinary study of the ancient Greek, Roman, Near Eastern, and Egyptian worlds through archaeology, history, literature, and religious texts.
Interdisciplinary examination of European and world history, culture, literature, religion, art, and philosophy from roughly 500–1700 CE.
Ancient Greek and Roman worlds through language (Greek and/or Latin), literature, history, philosophy, and material culture.
Interdisciplinary major on modern Europe's history, politics, literature, culture, and languages from national and transnational perspectives.

Social/cultural anthropology and archaeology. Social/cultural focuses on elites, gender, art, science, and medicine; archaeology explores human prehistory through material evidence and excavation, with notable fieldwork in Africa.

Interdisciplinary major studying perception, learning, memory, language, concept formation, decision-making, and consciousness across biological, psychological, linguistic, and philosophical perspectives. Specializations: Computation, Linguistics, Neuroscience, Philosophy, Psychology.
Microeconomics, macroeconomics, and econometrics applied to economic phenomena and public policy.

Interdepartmental program in global systems and organizations drawing on 13 academic departments. Emphasizes global competence, cross-cultural understanding via language study, and applied learning with practitioners. Overseen by Political Science with the School of Humanities and Arts.

Structure, history, and social use of language — cognitive/functional linguistics, phonetics, phonology, syntax, semantics, sociolinguistics, discourse analysis, and typology. Field studies in American Indian, Australian, Austronesian, and African languages.
MEOS combines economics, political science, and psychology to prepare students for organizational and business environments without a fully professional business degree.
MTEC — mathematically intensive program bridging Economics and Mathematics, recommended for graduate work in economics or analytical careers in business or government.
American politics, comparative politics, and international relations — political institutions, behavior, and public policy.
Social, cognitive, and affective neuroscience; health psychology; human factors and human-computer interaction; and industrial-organizational psychology.
Analysis of policy designed to improve human well-being. Students learn evidence-based policy design, quantitative evaluation, and communication, drawing on economics and political science.
How people interact in groups and societies — race and ethnic stratification, gender, religion, health disparities, and demographic change (migration, population shifts).
Quantitative and statistical analysis applied to performance, business, and operations in the sport industry. Jointly managed by the D2K Lab, Sport Management, and Statistics.

Interdisciplinary major in business, management, law, and communication for the sport industry. Concentrations: Sport Law; Sport Leadership. Houston internships with professional teams (Astros, Texans, Rockets, Dynamo).
Liberal-arts music degree covering music history, theory, performance, and composition alongside a broad Rice education, without the full professional conservatory requirements of the B.Mus.
Comprehensive grounding in the history and musicology of Western and world music traditions, with training in historical research and scholarly writing.
Analytical, compositional, and cognitive dimensions of music theory — counterpoint, harmony, form, 20th-century techniques, and computational/mathematical approaches.
Craft and artistry of composition across styles and media, with instruction in orchestration, electronic music, and new music practices.
Technical and interpretive skills of conducting orchestral and ensemble repertoire — score study, gesture, rehearsal technique, and musicianship.
Solo and accompanying organ repertoire from Baroque to contemporary, with technique, registration, and improvisation.
Conservatory training in solo piano repertoire, technique, and musicianship with chamber music and collaborative piano opportunities.
Conservatory study of violin repertoire and technique from Baroque to contemporary, with orchestral and chamber participation.
Viola repertoire, technique, and orchestral/chamber performance with attention to the viola's distinctive solo and ensemble literature.
Cello technique and the full span of cello repertoire, including chamber music and orchestral playing.
Solo, orchestral, and chamber music repertoire for double bass across styles.
Solo flute repertoire from Baroque to contemporary, with chamber and orchestral experience.
Oboe repertoire, reed-making, and orchestral and chamber playing.
Clarinet technique and repertoire across historical periods — orchestral, chamber, and solo.
Bassoon technique, reed-making, and bassoon repertoire in solo, chamber, and orchestral contexts.
French horn technique and orchestral, chamber, and solo repertoire.
Trumpet performance from Baroque to contemporary, including orchestral playing.
Trombone technique and repertoire in solo, jazz-influenced, orchestral, and chamber contexts.
Tuba technique and expanding solo and ensemble repertoire, including orchestral excerpts.
Harp technique and solo, orchestral, and chamber repertoire for pedal and lever harp.
Mallet instruments, timpani, snare drum, and world percussion across solo and ensemble repertoire.
Operatic and art-song singing — vocal technique, diction (Italian, German, French, English), music theory, and acting for singers. Supported by Brockman Hall for Opera.

Five-year NAAB-accredited professional program educating architects for practice and leadership. Graduates qualify to sit for state licensing exams after the required post-graduation internship. Includes the signature Rice Preceptorship.

Four-year liberal-arts degree in architectural history, theory, design, and culture without full professional accreditation. Flexible for students interested in architecture's relationship to urbanism, history, culture, and allied disciplines.

Foundational undergraduate business degree covering leadership and core business fundamentals — finance, economics, accounting, marketing, and communications. Optional concentrations in Finance, Management, and Marketing.
